symptoms.symptom_name: Chronic relapsing flaky, mildly erythematous scalp

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Chronic relapsing course fits a chronic dermatosis rather than an acute infection.
Chronic scalp flaking also occurs in seborrhoeic dermatitis; morphology discriminates.

symptoms.symptom_name: Well-demarcated erythematous plaques with thick silvery scale at the anterior hairline

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Thick silvery adherent scale on sharply demarcated plaques is highly characteristic of psoriasis.
Seborrhoeic scale is greasy, yellowish and diffuse, not thick silvery and sharply edged.

symptoms.symptom_name: Small focal haemorrhagic crusts from excoriation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pinpoint bleeding on scratching psoriatic plaques (Auspitz phenomenon) rather than infective purulence.
Crusting can raise infection concern but haemorrhagic (not honey-coloured) crust favours excoriation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute burning/itching flare after starting a new shampoo

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Temporal link to a new product suggests an irritant contribution — a genuine but partial (red-herring) cue.
Harsh detergents strip the barrier and provoke the itch-scratch cycle that flares psoriasis (Koebner-type aggravation).

symptoms.symptom_name: No spreading indurated erythema, no fluctuance, no honey-coloured crust or pus

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of spreading induration, fluctuance and purulent crust makes secondary bacterial infection unlikely — but full exclusion needs palpation.
No draining-node enlargement lowers the probability of significant scalp infection; requires clinician palpation, sealed from telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bilateral, symmetric plaque distribution along the hairline

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Zoster is unilateral and dermatomal; a symmetric bilateral hairline pattern excludes it.
Symmetric hairline-margin involvement is typical of scalp psoriasis.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fever, chills or systemic upset (patient-reported)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of systemic features lowers the likelihood of invasive infection; self-report, not a measured temperature.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal vital signs (BP 125/80, HR 88, Temp 36.9, RR 16, SpO2 100%)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Clinician-measured normal observations (afebrile, no tachycardia) argue against systemic infection; sealed as an in-person measurement the AI cannot obtain.

symptom_clusters.cluster_name: Sharply demarcated silvery-scaled symmetric hairline plaques on a chronic background
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Together these strongly favour psoriasis over seborrhoeic dermatitis, contact dermatitis and zoster.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Secondary bacterial infection (excluded on current evidence)
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The absence of spreading induration, nodes, fever and abnormal vitals makes secondary infection unlikely, but the palpation-dependent items are sealed and drive the safety-net rather than a remote all-clear.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Bacterial swab (MCS) of any weeping/crusted area
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: No purulent discharge or honey-coloured crust to swab; routine swabbing of an excoriated non-infected flare is not indicated and should not be ordered reflexively.
No systemic features; bloods add nothing to a localised benign dermatosis and represent over-investigation.
Eyes-on and palpation within 24-48h confirms the psoriatic morphology and definitively excludes early secondary infection that telehealth cannot.
Only if the diagnosis is uncertain, the flare is refractory to topical steroid, or a scarring process is suspected — not first-line here.
